diagrams.title: Virtual Address Space to Physical Memory Translation
diagrams.description: Shows how virtual addresses are translated to physical addresses using page tables
elements: CPU generating 32-bit virtual address (divided into page number and offset)
Page Table stored in main memory with valid/invalid bits, frame numbers, and protection bits
Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) as cache between CPU and page table
Physical memory frames with actual data/pages
Disk (swap space) for pages not in memory
MMU (Memory Management Unit) performing the translation
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes the complete virtual memory translation mechanism - essential for understanding how virtual memory provides illusion of large address space. Exam: Page table structure, TLB hit/miss, valid-invalid bits

diagrams.title: Demand Paging Flowchart
diagrams.description: Step-by-step process when CPU accesses a page not in memory
elements: CPU generates memory reference for virtual page
Check page table entry (valid bit = 0)
Page fault trap triggered
OS locates page in disk (swap space/file system)
Find free frame in physical memory
Read page from disk to free frame
Update page table (set valid bit, frame number)
Restart the interrupted instruction
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the complete page fault handling sequence. Exam: Page fault service time calculation, steps involved in handling page fault

diagrams.title: Page Replacement Algorithms Classification
diagrams.description: Shows all major page replacement algorithms and their logic
elements: FIFO (First In First Out) - oldest page replaced
Optimal (OPT) - future reference-based (theoretical benchmark)
LRU (Least Recently Used) - using stack algorithm property
Second Chance / Clock - circular queue with reference bit
Counting-based (LFU/MFU) - page with minimum/maximum use count
NRU (Not Recently Used) - classified by Reference and Modified bits
diagrams.purpose: Shows algorithm hierarchy and relationships. Exam: Belady's anomaly with FIFO, LRU stack property, Clock algorithm implementation

diagrams.title: Copy-on-Write Mechanism
diagrams.description: Process of lazy copying between parent and child processes in fork
elements: Parent process with pages in memory
fork system call creates child process
Initially both share same physical pages (read-only)
Write attempt triggers page fault
OS allocates new frame, copies original page
Page mapped to child's address space (read-write)
Parent's page remains unchanged
diagrams.purpose: Explains how fork efficiently duplicates processes. Exam: Memory efficiency, reduced fork overhead, when COW actually copies

diagrams.title: Thrashing Diagram
diagrams.description: Shows CPU utilization vs degree of multiprogramming and thrashing behavior
elements: X-axis: Degree of multiprogramming (number of processes)
Y-axis: CPU utilization percentage
Peak point: Optimal number of processes
Thrashing zone: Sharp decline in CPU utilization
Page fault frequency curve (inverse relationship)
Working set model visualization
diagrams.purpose: Explains thrashing concept and relationship with page fault frequency. Exam: Thrashing definition, causes, working set model, page fault frequency control

charts.title: Page Replacement Algorithms Comparison
charts.purpose: Quick reference for exam - compare algorithm properties, Belady's anomaly, practical implementation complexity

charts.title: Memory Request to Page Fault Handler Flow
charts.content: CPU Request → TLB Lookup → Page Table Check → (If valid: access frame) → (If invalid: Page Fault) → OS finds page in disk → Allocate frame → Read from disk → Update page table → Restart instruction
charts.purpose: Complete flow of memory access with and without page fault - essential for understanding page fault service time calculations

charts.title: Page Table Types Comparison

rows: Too large for large address spaces

charts.purpose: Compare different page table organizations - important for understanding memory overhead and translation speed

charts.title: Effective Access Time Calculation Factors

parameters: Page fault service time - typically 1-10 ms (disk I/O dominant)
formulas: EAT = (1-α) × (2 × memory access + page fault) + α × (TLB + memory)
Without TLB: EAT = memory access × 2 (page table + frame access)

charts.purpose: Exam formula reference - calculate effective memory access time considering TLB, page faults, and memory hierarchy

mindMaps.purpose: Complete overview connecting all virtual memory concepts - shows relationships between address translation, demand paging, page replacement, and performance optimization. Helps in answering 10-15 mark questions requiring integrated understanding
Detailed breakdown of page fault handling - forms basis for page fault rate and effective access time calculations in exam numericals
